preamble: The transformation from a voiced aspirated velar plosive /gʱ/ to a voiced postalveolar fricative /ʒ/ is a complex but phonetically plausible shift, typically occurring through a combination of palatalization and lenition. Palatalization shifts the place of articulation forward from the velum to the postalveolar region, often triggered by front vowels or palatal approximants. Concurrently, lenition reduces the stricture of the consonant, changing a plosive into a fricative. During this process, the aspiration feature of the original velar plosive is typically lost, as fricatives are generally unaspirated.
phoneticEffects: Palatalization, Lenition (Spirantization), Deaspiration, Fronting (Velar to Postalveolar)

examples.note: Palatalization of *g before *j, leading to an intermediate affricate *dž, then deaffrication to *ž. The aspiration of an original *gʱ would have been lost in this process.
Alternation of /g/ to /ʒ/ in the vocative singular of masculine nouns (e.g., 'God'). This reflects the historical first palatalization of velars.
Alternation of /g/ to /ʒ/ in verb conjugations (e.g., 'can', 2nd person singular present tense).
Alternation of /g/ to /ʒ/ in verb derivation (e.g., 'servant' to 'to serve').

sources: Slavic languages - Indo-European, Dialects, Grammar | Britannica
